skills.ref: AP World Pre-Course Context (before 1200 CE)
skills.prompt: This topic covers the Neolithic Revolution for AP World History students (AP World Pre-Course Context, before 1200 CE). Students should understand how the transition from foraging to agriculture fundamentally transformed human societies. Key concepts: the shift from nomadic hunter-gatherer lifestyles to settled agricultural communities beginning around 10,000 BCE; independent development of agriculture in multiple regions (Mesopotamia, China, Mesoamerica, sub-Saharan Africa); the domestication of plants (wheat, rice, maize, millet) and animals (sheep, cattle, horses, pigs); consequences of agriculture including population growth, permanent settlements, surplus food production, social stratification, division of labor, emergence of elites and priests, and new gender roles; the environmental impact of clearing land for farming. Students should analyze why agriculture arose independently in different regions and how it created the conditions for civilization. Vocabulary: Neolithic Revolution, domestication, sedentary, surplus, stratification, pastoralism, foraging, irrigation, specialization.

skills.ref: AP World Pre-Course Context (before 1200 CE)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Early Civilizations for AP World History students (AP World Pre-Course Context, before 1200 CE). Students should understand the characteristics and developments of the first complex societies. Key concepts: early civilizations arose in river valleys (Mesopotamia/Tigris-Euphrates, Egypt/Nile, Indus Valley, China/Yellow River); common features include cities, centralized government, social hierarchy, writing systems, specialized labor, monumental architecture, and organized religion; writing systems (cuneiform, hieroglyphics, oracle bones) enabled record-keeping and administration; legal codes (Code of Hammurabi) established formal justice; trade networks connected distant regions. Students should compare and contrast early civilizations, analyze how geography shaped their development, and understand that these societies established patterns (urbanization, record-keeping, social hierarchy) that persisted for millennia. Vocabulary: civilization, city-state, empire, writing system, cuneiform, hieroglyphics, social hierarchy, irrigation, polytheism, tribute.

skills.ref: AP World Pre-Course Context (before 1200 CE)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Classical Civilizations for AP World History students (AP World Pre-Course Context, before 1200 CE). Students should understand the major empires and cultural achievements of the classical period (approximately 600 BCE to 600 CE). Key concepts: major classical civilizations include Persia (Achaemenid, Sasanid), Greece (democracy, philosophy), Rome (republic to empire, law, engineering), Maurya and Gupta India (Hinduism, Buddhism, caste system), Han China (Confucianism, bureaucracy, Silk Road trade); common features include centralized administration, road systems, trade networks, monumental architecture, and cultural golden ages; the Silk Road and Indian Ocean trade connected Afro-Eurasia; the spread of universal religions (Buddhism, Christianity, later Islam); reasons for decline (overexpansion, invasions, internal decay, disease). Students should compare classical empires and analyze how trade and cultural exchange connected distant societies. Vocabulary: classical, empire, republic, democracy, philosophy, Silk Road, caste, Confucianism, Buddhism, Pax Romana, bureaucracy.

skills.ref: AP World Unit 1: The Global Tapestry (1200-1450), Topics 1.2-1.6
skills.prompt: This topic covers the Rise of Major Religions for AP World History students (AP World Unit 1: The Global Tapestry, 1200-1450, Topics 1.2-1.6). Students should understand how major belief systems spread and shaped societies across Afro-Eurasia. Key concepts: Buddhism spread from India through Central Asia along the Silk Road to China, Korea, and Japan, adapting to local cultures (Mahayana, Theravada, Zen); Islam emerged in 7th-century Arabia and spread rapidly through trade, conquest, and missionary activity across the Middle East, North Africa, sub-Saharan Africa, and South/Southeast Asia; Christianity spread through missionaries, trade networks, and political adoption (Roman Empire, later medieval Europe); Hinduism shaped South and Southeast Asian cultures through concepts of dharma, karma, and caste. Students should analyze how religions adapted to local contexts, facilitated cross-cultural exchange, and provided social structures and moral frameworks. Vocabulary: monotheism, polytheism, missionary, conversion, syncretic, dar al-Islam, Sharia, dharma, karma, Confucianism, Daoism.

skills.ref: AP World Unit 1: The Global Tapestry (1200-1450)
skills.prompt: This topic covers the Post-Classical Era for AP World History students (AP World Unit 1: The Global Tapestry, 1200-1450). Students should understand the diverse political, economic, and cultural developments across regions during the medieval period. Key concepts: Song Dynasty China (technological innovation, Neo-Confucianism, commercialization, urbanization); the Islamic Golden Age (scholarship in math, science, medicine, philosophy; Abbasid Caliphate; trade networks); Byzantine Empire (preservation of Roman/Greek learning, Orthodox Christianity, Justinian's Code); Medieval Europe (feudalism, manorialism, Catholic Church dominance, Crusades); Mongol Empire (largest contiguous land empire, facilitated trade and cultural exchange across Eurasia, Pax Mongolica); West African kingdoms (Ghana, Mali, Songhai; gold-salt trade); South and Southeast Asian kingdoms. Students should compare state-building and cultural development across regions. AP skills: comparison, contextualization. Vocabulary: feudalism, caliphate, Song Dynasty, Mongol Empire, Pax Mongolica, Crusades, manor, guild, Islamic Golden Age.

skills.ref: AP World Unit 4: Transoceanic Interconnections (1450-1750), Topics 4.1-4.3
skills.prompt: This topic covers the Age of Exploration for AP World History students (AP World Unit 4: Transoceanic Interconnections, 1450-1750, Topics 4.1-4.3). Students should understand how European maritime exploration created the first truly global connections. Key concepts: motivations for exploration (spices, gold, spreading Christianity, competition among European states); Portuguese exploration of Africa's coast and the Indian Ocean trade; Columbus's voyages and the Spanish colonization of the Americas; the Columbian Exchange (plants, animals, diseases, peoples transferred between hemispheres); the devastating impact of Old World diseases on indigenous American populations; the Atlantic slave trade and the triangular trade system; the encomienda and plantation systems; how colonialism transformed the Americas, Africa, and Europe. Students should analyze both the motivations and consequences of exploration, including how it created new global economic patterns and power dynamics. Vocabulary: Columbian Exchange, triangular trade, Middle Passage, encomienda, colonialism, mercantilism, joint-stock company, caravel, plantation.

skills.ref: AP World Unit 3: Land-Based Empires (1450-1750), Topic 3.1
skills.prompt: This topic covers the Renaissance and Reformation for AP World History students (AP World Unit 3: Land-Based Empires, 1450-1750, Topic 3.1). Students should understand how intellectual and religious movements transformed European society. Key concepts: the Renaissance was a cultural rebirth centered in Italian city-states that revived classical Greek and Roman learning; humanism emphasized human potential, individualism, and secular concerns alongside religious ones; artistic innovations included perspective, realism, and portraiture (da Vinci, Michelangelo, Raphael); the printing press (Gutenberg, c.1450) revolutionized information dissemination; the Protestant Reformation began with Martin Luther's 95 Theses (1517) challenging Catholic practices (indulgences, papal authority); the Reformation fragmented Western Christianity (Lutheranism, Calvinism, Anglicanism); the Catholic Counter-Reformation (Council of Trent, Jesuits); religious wars devastated Europe. Students should analyze how these movements challenged traditional authority and shaped modern Western thought. Vocabulary: Renaissance, humanism, Reformation, Protestant, printing press, indulgence, 95 Theses, Counter-Reformation, secular, individualism.

skills.ref: AP World Unit 5: Revolutions (1750-1900), Topic 5.1
skills.prompt: This topic covers the Scientific Revolution for AP World History students (AP World Unit 5: Revolutions, 1750-1900, Topic 5.1). Students should understand how a new approach to understanding the natural world emerged in Europe during the 16th-18th centuries. Key concepts: the shift from reliance on ancient authorities (Aristotle, Ptolemy, Church teachings) to empirical observation, experimentation, and mathematical reasoning; key figures include Copernicus (heliocentric model), Galileo (telescope observations, experimental method), Kepler (planetary motion), Newton (gravity, laws of motion, calculus); Francis Bacon championed inductive reasoning and the scientific method; Descartes promoted deductive reasoning and rational skepticism; new institutions (Royal Society, academies) supported scientific inquiry. Students should understand how the Scientific Revolution challenged traditional authority, laid the groundwork for the Enlightenment, and transformed humanity's understanding of nature. Vocabulary: Scientific Revolution, empiricism, heliocentric, geocentric, scientific method, inductive reasoning, deductive reasoning, hypothesis, experimentation.

skills.ref: AP World Unit 5: Revolutions (1750-1900), Topic 5.1
skills.prompt: This topic covers the Enlightenment for AP World History students (AP World Unit 5: Revolutions, 1750-1900, Topic 5.1). Students should understand how Enlightenment thinkers applied reason and natural law to questions of government, society, and human rights. Key concepts: key thinkers include John Locke (natural rights: life, liberty, property; social contract; consent of the governed), Montesquieu (separation of powers), Voltaire (freedom of speech and religion), Rousseau (general will, direct democracy), Mary Wollstonecraft (women's rights); the idea that reason, not tradition or divine right, should guide society; the concept of natural rights that governments must protect; influence on the American Revolution, French Revolution, and Latin American independence movements; salon culture and the Republic of Letters spread Enlightenment ideas. Students should analyze how Enlightenment ideas challenged absolute monarchy and shaped modern democratic thought and human rights frameworks. Vocabulary: Enlightenment, natural rights, social contract, reason, philosophe, salon, deism, natural law, separation of powers, consent.

skills.ref: AP World Unit 5: Revolutions (1750-1900), Topic 5.2
skills.prompt: This topic covers the French Revolution for AP World History students (AP World Unit 5: Revolutions, 1750-1900, Topic 5.2). Students should understand the causes, phases, and global impact of the French Revolution (1789-1799). Key concepts: causes include Enlightenment ideas, financial crisis, social inequality (Three Estates system), weak leadership of Louis XVI, and the example of the American Revolution; major events include the storming of the Bastille, the Declaration of the Rights of Man and Citizen, the abolition of feudal privileges, and the execution of the king; the revolution's radical phase (Reign of Terror under Robespierre, Committee of Public Safety); Napoleon's rise from the revolution's aftermath; the revolution's global impact on independence movements in Haiti, Latin America, and later Europe. Students should analyze the tension between revolutionary ideals (liberty, equality, fraternity) and the violence used to achieve them. AP skills: causation, comparison. Vocabulary: revolution, Bastille, Reign of Terror, Declaration of Rights of Man, Jacobin, Napoleon, Estates-General, republic, radical.

skills.ref: AP World Unit 6: Consequences of Industrialization (1750-1900), Topics 6.1-6.4
skills.prompt: This topic covers the Industrial Revolution for AP World History students (AP World Unit 6: Consequences of Industrialization, 1750-1900, Topics 6.1-6.4). Students should understand how industrialization transformed economies, societies, and global power dynamics. Key concepts: origins in Britain (coal, iron, textile manufacturing, steam power, factory system); key innovations (spinning jenny, water frame, steam engine, railroads); factors enabling British industrialization (capital, natural resources, stable government, agricultural surplus, colonial markets); the factory system replaced cottage industry, creating a new working class; urbanization brought overcrowding, pollution, and public health crises; social consequences including child labor, women's changing roles, and the emergence of the middle class; responses to industrialization (labor unions, socialism, Marxism, reform legislation); industrialization spread to Western Europe, the United States, Russia, and Japan. Vocabulary: industrialization, factory system, urbanization, proletariat, bourgeoisie, capitalism, socialism, Marxism, steam engine, labor union.

skills.ref: AP World Unit 6: Consequences of Industrialization (1750-1900), Topics 6.2-6.5
skills.prompt: This topic covers Imperialism and Colonialism for AP World History students (AP World Unit 6: Consequences of Industrialization, 1750-1900, Topics 6.2-6.5). Students should understand how industrialized nations established control over vast territories in Africa, Asia, and the Pacific. Key concepts: motivations include economic gain (raw materials, markets), strategic competition, nationalism, Social Darwinism, and the 'civilizing mission' ideology; forms of imperial control (colonies, protectorates, spheres of influence, economic imperialism); the Scramble for Africa (Berlin Conference 1884-85 divided Africa among European powers); British India as a case study of colonial administration; resistance to imperialism (Sepoy Rebellion, Zulu Wars, Boxer Rebellion, Ethiopian victory at Adowa); consequences including economic exploitation, cultural disruption, artificial borders, and the creation of colonial racial hierarchies. Students should analyze imperialism from multiple perspectives (colonizers and colonized). Vocabulary: imperialism, colonialism, Scramble for Africa, Berlin Conference, protectorate, sphere of influence, Social Darwinism, resistance, exploitation.

skills.ref: AP World Unit 7: Global Conflict (1900-Present), Topics 7.1-7.2
skills.prompt: This topic covers World War I for AP World History students (AP World Unit 7: Global Conflict, 1900-Present, Topics 7.1-7.2). Students should understand the causes, conduct, and consequences of the first truly global industrial war. Key concepts: long-term causes (alliance systems, militarism, imperialism, nationalism); the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand as the immediate trigger; trench warfare, new military technology (machine guns, poison gas, tanks, aircraft), and unprecedented casualties; the war's global scope (European, Middle Eastern, African, and Asian theaters); the Russian Revolution (1917) during the war; total war mobilizing entire societies (women in workforce, propaganda, rationing); the Treaty of Versailles (war guilt clause, reparations, territorial changes, League of Nations); the Ottoman Empire's collapse and the mandate system; the war's legacy of disillusionment and instability. Students should analyze how WWI reshaped the global order and planted seeds for future conflicts. Vocabulary: alliance, militarism, imperialism, nationalism, trench warfare, total war, Treaty of Versailles, mandate, armistice, reparations.

skills.ref: AP World Unit 7: Global Conflict (1900-Present), Topics 7.5-7.6
skills.prompt: This topic covers World War II for AP World History students (AP World Unit 7: Global Conflict, 1900-Present, Topics 7.5-7.6). Students should understand the causes, global scope, and transformative consequences of World War II. Key concepts: causes include the Treaty of Versailles grievances, global depression, rise of fascism and militarism (Nazi Germany, Imperial Japan, Fascist Italy), failure of appeasement; the war involved every continent and resulted in 70-85 million deaths; the Holocaust and other genocides; key turning points (Battle of Stalingrad, D-Day, Midway, atomic bombs); the home front (total war economies, propaganda, women's roles, colonial troops); the war's aftermath (United Nations, Bretton Woods system, beginning of the Cold War, decolonization, Nuremberg Trials); the emergence of the United States and Soviet Union as superpowers. Students should analyze how WWII reshaped the global order and created the framework for the post-war world. Vocabulary: fascism, appeasement, Holocaust, genocide, total war, atomic bomb, United Nations, superpower, Nuremberg, occupation.

skills.ref: AP World Unit 8: Cold War and Decolonization (1900-Present), Topics 8.1-8.3
skills.prompt: This topic covers the Cold War for AP World History students (AP World Unit 8: Cold War and Decolonization, 1900-Present, Topics 8.1-8.3). Students should understand the ideological, political, and military rivalry between the U.S.-led capitalist West and the Soviet-led communist East. Key concepts: origins in ideological differences and post-WWII power vacuums; the division of Europe (Iron Curtain, NATO vs. Warsaw Pact); proxy wars (Korea, Vietnam, Afghanistan, Angola, Latin America); the nuclear arms race and mutually assured destruction (MAD); the Cuban Missile Crisis as the closest approach to nuclear war; the Non-Aligned Movement (countries refusing to join either bloc); the Cold War's impact on developing nations (superpower interventions, coups, economic aid as leverage); the Space Race; detente and eventual Soviet collapse (1991). Students should analyze how the Cold War shaped global politics, economics, and culture for nearly half a century. Vocabulary: Cold War, containment, proxy war, Iron Curtain, NATO, Warsaw Pact, nuclear arms race, MAD, Non-Aligned Movement, detente.

skills.ref: AP World Unit 8: Cold War and Decolonization (1900-Present), Topics 8.4-8.8
skills.prompt: This topic covers Decolonization for AP World History students (AP World Unit 8: Cold War and Decolonization, 1900-Present, Topics 8.4-8.8). Students should understand how colonized peoples achieved independence and the challenges they faced in building new nations. Key concepts: factors driving decolonization include WWII weakening colonial powers, Cold War competition for allies, growing nationalist movements inspired by Enlightenment ideals and Marxism, and UN advocacy for self-determination; paths to independence varied from negotiated transfers of power (India, 1947) to armed struggle (Algeria, Vietnam, Angola); Partition of India and its consequences; the challenges of nation-building (artificial colonial borders, ethnic/religious divisions, economic dependency, Cold War interference); neo-colonialism (continued economic and political influence by former colonial powers); Pan-Africanism and Pan-Arabism as unifying movements. Students should analyze how colonial legacies shaped post-independence challenges. Vocabulary: decolonization, nationalism, self-determination, partition, neo-colonialism, Pan-Africanism, non-violent resistance, armed struggle.

skills.ref: AP World Unit 9: Globalization (1900-Present), Topics 9.1-9.6
skills.prompt: This topic covers Globalization for AP World History students (AP World Unit 9: Globalization, 1900-Present, Topics 9.1-9.6). Students should understand the increasing interconnection of the world's economies, cultures, and populations in the late 20th and early 21st centuries. Key concepts: economic globalization (free trade agreements like NAFTA and WTO, multinational corporations, outsourcing, global supply chains); technological drivers (Internet, telecommunications, container shipping, air travel); cultural globalization (spread of Western popular culture, media, and consumer brands, but also cultural resistance and localization); political dimensions (international organizations, NGOs, transnational challenges); debates over globalization's effects (economic growth vs. inequality, cultural exchange vs. homogenization, environmental impact); anti-globalization movements. Students should analyze who benefits from and who is harmed by globalization, evaluating multiple perspectives. Vocabulary: globalization, free trade, multinational corporation, outsourcing, WTO, NAFTA, cultural diffusion, homogenization, interconnection, NGO.

skills.ref: AP World Units 7-9: Global Conflict through Globalization
skills.prompt: This topic covers 20th and 21st Century Conflicts for AP World History students (AP World Units 7-9: Global Conflict through Globalization). Students should understand the major conflicts that have shaped the modern world beyond the two World Wars and the Cold War. Key concepts: regional conflicts (Arab-Israeli conflict, Iran-Iraq War, Rwandan genocide, Yugoslav Wars); terrorism and the War on Terror (9/11, al-Qaeda, ISIS); civil wars and failed states (Syria, Somalia, Congo); nuclear proliferation concerns (North Korea, Iran); ethnic and religious conflicts; humanitarian crises and refugee movements; the role of international organizations (UN peacekeeping, ICC) in responding to conflict; the relationship between resource competition and conflict; the impact of technology on modern warfare (drones, cyber warfare). Students should analyze patterns of conflict, evaluate responses, and understand the human costs of violence. AP skills: causation, contextualization. Vocabulary: genocide, terrorism, civil war, refugee, humanitarian crisis, peacekeeping, proliferation, ethnic conflict, intervention.

skills.ref: AP World Unit 9: Globalization (1900-Present), Topic 9.5
skills.prompt: This topic covers Global Economic Development for AP World History students (AP World Unit 9: Globalization, 1900-Present, Topic 9.5). Students should understand the uneven patterns of economic development across the world and efforts to address global inequality. Key concepts: the divide between developed and developing nations; economic models (capitalism, socialism, mixed economies); international financial institutions (World Bank, International Monetary Fund, WTO) and their influence on developing nations; structural adjustment programs and their controversial effects; the Asian economic miracle (Japan, South Korea, China, India); oil-producing nations and OPEC; the debt crisis in developing nations; microfinance and development aid debates; the role of education, healthcare, and infrastructure in economic development; the UN Sustainable Development Goals. Students should analyze why economic development has been uneven and evaluate different approaches to promoting growth and reducing poverty. Vocabulary: development, GDP, industrialization, structural adjustment, microfinance, World Bank, IMF, OPEC, developing nation, economic growth.

skills.ref: AP World Unit 9: Globalization (1900-Present), Topics 9.3-9.6
skills.prompt: This topic covers Contemporary Global Issues for AP World History students (AP World Unit 9: Globalization, 1900-Present, Topics 9.3-9.6). Students should understand the major challenges facing the world in the 21st century. Key concepts: climate change and environmental degradation (deforestation, pollution, biodiversity loss, rising sea levels); global health challenges (pandemics including COVID-19, HIV/AIDS, access to healthcare); migration and refugee crises driven by conflict, economic opportunity, and climate change; human rights issues (gender equality, LGBTQ rights, freedom of expression, modern slavery); technology and its dual-edged impact (social media, artificial intelligence, surveillance, digital divide); income inequality within and between nations; food security and water scarcity; the challenge of international cooperation on transnational problems. Students should analyze how these interconnected issues require global solutions and how historical patterns inform current challenges. Vocabulary: climate change, pandemic, migration, human rights, sustainability, digital divide, food security, inequality, transnational.
